name: Diabetes
what_it_is: Diabetes is a condition where blood sugar (glucose) stays too high. Type 2 is common in adults and often linked to lifestyle; Type 1 usually starts in childhood. Good control reduces complications and healthcare costs.
preventive_lifestyle: Maintain a healthy weight
Regular physical activity (e.g. 150 min/week moderate exercise)
Eat more whole grains, vegetables, and fibre; limit sugary and highly processed foods
Get enough sleep and manage stress
Avoid smoking; limit alcohol
when_to_see_doctor: See a doctor if you have persistent thirst, frequent urination, unexplained tiredness, or blurred vision. Get regular blood sugar checks if you have risk factors. Early detection helps prevent complications.
source_notes: Align with WHO and national guidelines; for education only, not diagnosis.

name: Hypertension
what_it_is: Hypertension means your blood pressure is consistently too high. It often has no obvious symptoms but increases risk of heart disease, stroke, and kidney problems. Managing it early reduces long-term cost and harm.
early_symptoms: Shortness of breath in severe cases
preventive_lifestyle: Reduce salt intake
Eat more fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy
Regular physical activity
Maintain healthy weight; avoid smoking and excess alcohol
Manage stress; get enough sleep
when_to_see_doctor: Get your blood pressure checked regularly (at least once a year if normal). See a doctor if readings are repeatedly high (e.g. 140/90 or above) or if you have headaches, dizziness, or chest discomfort.
source_notes: WHO and national BP guidelines; education only.

name: Heart disease
what_it_is: Heart (cardiovascular) disease includes conditions that affect the heart and blood vessels, such as coronary artery disease. Early prevention through lifestyle and risk control can reduce the need for expensive treatments later.
early_symptoms: Shortness of breath, especially on exertion
Swelling in legs or feet in some cases
risk_factors: Smoking, unhealthy diet, lack of exercise
preventive_lifestyle: Quit smoking; avoid second-hand smoke
Eat a heart-friendly diet (less salt, saturated fat; more vegetables, whole grains)
Regular physical activity
Control BP, blood sugar, and cholesterol with doctor's guidance
Manage stress and maintain healthy weight
when_to_see_doctor: See a doctor if you have chest pain, unexplained shortness of breath, or palpitations. Get regular check-ups if you have risk factors. In an emergency (severe chest pain, difficulty breathing), seek immediate care.

name: Respiratory infections
what_it_is: Respiratory infections affect the nose, throat, and sometimes lungs. Most colds and mild flu get better on their own. Knowing when to rest and when to see a doctor helps avoid unnecessary tests and medicines.
early_symptoms: Runny or stuffy nose, sore throat
In flu: higher fever, chills, more severe body ache
preventive_lifestyle: Hand washing; avoid touching face with unwashed hands
Cover mouth when coughing or sneezing
Stay away from sick people when possible
Flu vaccine if recommended by doctor
Good sleep, balanced diet, hydration
when_to_see_doctor: See a doctor if fever is high or lasting more than 3 days, breathing is difficult, or you have severe throat pain or prolonged cough. Children and elderly with symptoms should be evaluated sooner.
source_notes: General health education; not a substitute for medical advice.

name: Thyroid disorders
what_it_is: The thyroid gland controls metabolism. Underactive (hypothyroidism) or overactive (hyperthyroidism) thyroid can cause fatigue, weight changes, and other symptoms. Simple blood tests and treatment can manage it effectively.
early_symptoms: Hypothyroidism: tiredness, weight gain, feeling cold, dry skin, constipation
Hyperthyroidism: weight loss, nervousness, fast heartbeat, sweating, heat intolerance
preventive_lifestyle: Adequate iodine in diet (use iodised salt as advised)
Regular check-ups if family history or symptoms
Avoid self-medicating with thyroid medicines; get proper diagnosis
when_to_see_doctor: See a doctor if you have persistent fatigue, unexplained weight change, feeling too cold or too hot, or palpitations. A simple blood test (TSH) can screen for thyroid problems.
source_notes: General education; diagnosis and treatment by doctor.
